system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task G-10: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow (extended)

## Context

Same as Task G: regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks of overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage protocol within 7 days. Ten candidate protocols instead of the original 5. **Choose one as the adopted protocol.**

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival** - SOFA score + 28-day survival prediction as sole ranking.

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome** - SOFA + QALY-weighted post-recovery prediction.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor** - procedural fairness, minimal discretion, no withdrawal except medical futility.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties** - survival 40%, life-cycle 20%, instrumental value 20%, pandemic factors 20%, lottery on ties.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** - small list of medical exclusions, then daily lottery among eligible.

**Option F: Patient-and-family co-decision** - admission requires multidisciplinary team + patient/proxy decision. Withdrawal requires consensus including family. Outcomes: dignified; aligns with autonomy ethics; reduces moral injury via shared responsibility. Risks: slow; emotional burden on families; outcomes depend on family resources; resource-intensive for clinical team.

**Option G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits** - everyone admitted for trial period (e.g., 5-7 days). Withdrawal only after pre-defined time without improvement, evaluated against pre-defined criteria. Outcomes: avoids initial exclusion; transparent timeline; ethically defensible. Risks: still requires withdrawal decisions; can prolong suffering; uses bed days that might save more lives elsewhere.

**Option H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity** - dynamic protocol: at <80% capacity use Option C, 80-95% switch to Option A, >95% switch to Option D. Re-evaluate weekly. Outcomes: adaptive to actual scarcity; matches ethical framework to severity; precedent in disaster medicine. Risks: complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments.

**Option I: Community-deliberated framework** - convene a citizen jury (25-40 community members, diverse) over a weekend to deliberate and propose criteria. Adopt their recommendation. Outcomes: democratic legitimacy; reflects community values; precedent in Oregon health care priorities. Risks: takes >7 days; jury may produce internally inconsistent framework; may produce framework medical leadership cannot endorse.

**Option J: Pre-pandemic preference registry** - patients pre-register their preferences for ICU care under scarcity (full intervention vs comfort-focused). During overflow, registered preferences guide allocation. Default for unregistered = full intervention. Outcomes: respects individual autonomy ex ante; transparent; preserves trust. Risks: registration uptake will be biased (well-resourced patients register more); changes nature of medical encounter into bureaucratic; doesn't apply to acute presentations.

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
Adopt **Option D**. In a sustained crisis, clinicians need more than a medical score; they need a shield. A multi-principle scoring system—weighted toward survival but incorporating life-cycle, instrumental value, and pandemic factors—translates competing ethical duties into a single, repeatable triage order. It forces the system to make values explicit rather than hiding them behind a veneer of clinical objectivity. Most importantly, the lottery on ties removes the impossible burden of tie-breaking from exhausted bedside staff, transforming an agonizing personal judgment into a defensible structural process. Within seven days, a small triage committee can operationalize this as a worksheet scored by an off-floor officer, insulating frontline caregivers from moral injury.

The most compelling alternative is Option A, which offers the seductive clarity of SOFA and 28-day survival alone. But collapsing triage into a single prognostic metric is neither neutral nor sustainable. It systematically disadvantages younger patients with reversible multi-system decompensation, pregnant patients whose physiology distorts severity scores, and essential workers whose recovery serves the broader community. Over six weeks, a purely utilitarian protocol will erode public trust and staff morale because it presents hard choices as mere math, erasing the pluralistic values a community expects its health system to honor.

The case I would regret is a 22-year-old single mother with severe but potentially reversible ARDS whose survival score falls just below the cutoff, and who then loses a lottery tie for the last bed to an older patient with a marginally better prognosis. Her death on the medical floor would haunt me because the protocol’s probabilistic logic—defensible in aggregate—cannot console in the particular. Yet that grief is precisely why the lottery is essential: without it, her death would be attributed to a clinician’s subjective whim; with it, the tragedy is borne by the system we chose together.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to recommend one ICU triage protocol from Options A-J for a regional health system under sustained capacity overflow (240 beds, 6 weeks, exhausted staff, need formal protocol within 7 days).

 Constraints:
 - 200-400 words
 - Advocate; do not enumerate
 - Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.
 - Do not enumerate all options.

 I need to pick ONE and defend it. I should not list pros/cons of all options. I should write a cohesive argument.

 Which option is best?
 Context: Sustained overflow, exhausted staff, 7 days to implement. Need operationalizable, ethically defensible, reduces moral injury.

 Let's evaluate:
 - Option A (SOFA + 28-day): Too blunt. SOFA alone misses many things. Pure medical outcome but doesn't account for equity/life-cycle.
 - Option B (QALY): Discriminates against disabled/elderly. Very contentious.
 - Option C (FCFS): Unethical in sustained crisis; doesn't maximize lives; beds go to those who happen to arrive first.
 - Option D (Multi-principle + lottery): Complex but comprehensive. 40% survival, 20% life-cycle, 20% instrumental value, 20% pandemic factors. Lottery on ties. This is ethically robust and used in some frameworks (e.g., UMich). But is it too complex for exhausted staff in 7 days?
 - Option E (Exclusion + lottery): Simple. But randomness after minimal exclusion feels capricious and may not maximize survival.
 - Option F (Co-decision): Too slow, resource-intensive. Bad for exhausted staff and 7-day timeline. Also autonomy doesn't solve scarcity.
 - Option G (Trial-of-therapy): Very appealing operationally? But under sustained 6-week overflow, admitting everyone for 5-7 days trial consumes scarce beds that could go to salvageable patients. Prolongs suffering.
 - Option H (Tiered): Adaptive. Matches disaster medicine precedent. Complex to implement but staff already know FCFS (tier 1), SOFA (tier 2), and multi-principle (tier 3) is the triage committee's job. Tier transitions are politically explosive but this is realistic. It defers the hardest choices until >95% capacity. At <80%, just normal operations (FCFS effectively). This seems very practical. But is 7 days enough? Maybe if tier 1 is just current practice, tier 2 is SOFA score (simple), tier 3 is committee triage (multi-principle).
 - Option I (Citizen jury): Takes >7 days. Cannot meet deadline.
 - Option J (Registry): Biased uptake; doesn't work for acute presentations. 7 days impossible to set up.

 So best candidates are D, H, or maybe A.

 But wait: "Sustained capacity overflow" for 6 weeks. Exhausted staff. Need formal protocol within 7 days.

 Option H seems strong because it doesn't force staff to do hard triage until absolutely necessary (>95%). At 80-95%, simple SOFA score (Option A) is used. This reduces burden and training time. It only uses the complex multi-principle scoring when things are truly catastrophic. It acknowledges that ethical frameworks should match severity of scarcity. This is smart.

 However, is Option H too complex? The prompt says risks are "complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments." But the context is 7 days to formalize. Option H could be formalized as a policy document quickly, even if staff training takes longer. But maybe the complexity makes it hard to adopt *within 7 days*.

 Option D (Multi-principle scoring) is a single protocol that handles all cases. It's complex but static. It gives clear guidance via a scoring rubric. Lottery on ties removes discretion and blame from clinicians. It balances utility (survival 40%, instrumental 20%), equity (life-cycle 20%), and public health (pandemic factors 20%). This is the most ethically defensible single framework. But is it feasible for exhausted staff? The lottery on ties is crucial because it removes agonizing tie-breaking from bedside clinicians.

 Actually, looking at disaster medicine and real-world triage (e.g., SNY, Penn), multi-principle frameworks are often recommended. Option D explicitly includes lottery to resolve ties, which protects staff from moral injury.

 What about Option G? Trial of therapy. Under sustained 6-week overflow, giving *everyone* a 5-7 day trial is a disaster. It consumes beds. It's not viable.

 What about Option A? Solely SOFA + 28-day survival. This is purely utilitarian and prognostic. The "most compelling alternative" might be Option A because it's simple and operational. But it ignores equity (age, life-cycle, instrumental value) and discriminates against chronically ill. Option D is better because it broadens the ethical basis.

 What about Option C (FCFS)? No, it's clearly bad.

 What about Option F? Too slow.

 So I think I will recommend **Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties**.

 Why: It is the only option that operationalizes a pluralistic ethical consensus without abdicating clinical judgment entirely. Survival probability anchors utility, while life-cycle and instrumental value address equity and reciprocity; pandemic factors acknowledge the specific crisis. The lottery on ties is critical: it removes the burden of tie-breaking from exhausted clinicians, reducing moral injury and second-guessing. It can be reduced to a worksheet or algorithm that a triage officer uses, creating distance between the scoring and the bedside nurse/doctor. This is feasible within 7 days if a small ethics/clinical team builds the rubric.

 Why not the most compelling alternative: The most compelling alternative is Option A (short-term survival) because it is simple and maximizes lives saved. However, survival prediction alone systematically disadvantages younger patients with reversible single-system failure (who might have slightly lower initial SOFA but huge long-term prognosis), elderly with high SOFA from chronic illness, and essential workers/healthcare staff (instrumental value). It collapses a multidimensional tragedy into a unidimensional score, violating distributive justice. Sustained overflow demands a framework communities can trust as fair, not merely efficient.

 Regret case: A 24-year-old with severe asthma and COVID who scores poorly on the survival component due to high initial SOFA, loses a lottery tie, and is denied admission. Days later, a 65-year-old with moderate illness who scored higher is discharged from ICU. The young patient dies on the medical floor. I would regret that the 20% weighting for life-cycle and instrumental value could not overcome the survival deficit for a young person with a potentially reversible condition, and that the lottery—while necessary for staff protection—felt arbitrary in the face of such a life. But I would still defend the protocol because without the lottery, that death would have been blamed on a specific clinician; the protocol converts an unbearable personal choice into a bearable structural sorrow.

 Wait, I need to check word count. Let's draft and adjust.

 Let's refine the argument.

 Alternative: What if I recommend Option H? It's very clever. But the prompt says "Choose one as the adopted protocol." Option H's complexity and political explosiveness at tier transitions during sustained overflow might be fatal. Also, implementing 3 protocols in 7 days is harder than 1.

 I think Option D is the strongest recommendation for an *analyst* because it represents a formal, defensible ethical framework. Option H is more of a meta-protocol.

 However, the user might expect me to consider feasibility. Let's look at Option D risks: not explicitly listed, but implied complexity. I can argue that the 7-day timeline is sufficient to operationalize a scoring rubric and train triage officers, whereas staff are already making these decisions implicitly and chaotically. Formalizing it reduces burden.

 Let's reconsider Option G. "Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits." Under sustained overflow, this seems like a non-starter because it admits everyone. The beds would be full of dying patients on day 5-7. It's not viable.

 Option F is also non-viable due to time/consensus requirements.

 So Option D it is.

 Structure:
 - Open with strong advocacy for D.
 - Explain why: pluralistic ethics, operationalizable as algorithm, lottery protects staff from moral injury.
 - Address why not Option A (most compelling alternative): pure survival scoring is simpler but sacrifices justice/equity and fails to maintain public trust over 6 weeks. It treats a complex moral decision as a simple lab value.
 - Address regret case: concrete, poignant example that tests the limits. Perhaps a young parent with borderline survival who loses a lottery tie. This shows I understand the cost.

 Let's write.

 Word count target: 200-400. Be concise but forceful. No bullet points. No enumeration of options.

 Draft:

 Adopt Option D. In a sustained crisis, clinicians need more than a medical score; they need a shield. A multi-principle scoring system—weighted toward survival but incorporating life-cycle, instrumental value, and pandemic factors—translates competing ethical duties into a single, repeatable triage order. It forces the system to make values explicit rather than hiding them behind a veneer of clinical objectivity. Most importantly, the lottery on ties removes the impossible burden of tie-breaking from exhausted bedside staff, transforming an agonizing personal judgment into a defensible structural process. Within seven days, a small triage committee can operationalize this as a worksheet scored by an off-floor officer, insulating frontline caregivers from moral injury.

 The most compelling alternative is Option A, which offers the seductive clarity of SOFA and 28-day survival alone. But collapsing triage into a single prognostic metric is neither neutral nor sustainable. It systematically disadvantages younger patients with reversible multi-system decompensation, pregnant patients whose physiology distorts severity scores, and essential workers whose recovery serves the broader community. Over six weeks, a purely utilitarian protocol will erode public trust and staff morale because it presents hard choices as mere math, erasing the pluralistic values a community expects its health system to honor.

 The case I would regret is a 22-year-old single mother with severe but potentially reversible ARDS whose survival score falls just below the cutoff, and who then loses a lottery tie for the last bed to an older patient with a marginally better prognosis. Her death on the medical floor would haunt me because the protocol’s probabilistic logic—defensible in aggregate—cannot console in the particular. Yet that grief is precisely why the lottery is essential: without it, her death would be attributed to a clinician’s subjective whim; with it, the tragedy is borne by the system we chose together.

 Let's count words. ~270 words. Good.

 Check against instructions:
 - Choose one as adopted protocol. (Yes, Option D)
 - Provide 200-400 words. (Yes, ~270)
 - Advocate; do not enumerate. (Yes, it's a flowing argument, not a list)
 - Address: why this protocol (yes), why not the most compelling alternative (yes, Option A), what specific patient case would make you regret this choice (yes, 22-year-old mother).
 - Do not enumerate all options. (I only mention D and A).

 This looks solid.

 Let me double check: does the prompt say "why not the most compelling alternative"? Yes. I identified Option A as the most compelling alternative (simple, survival-focused). I explained why not.

 Is there any other compelling alternative? Option H is compelling too. But Option A is the standard utilitarian medical approach and likely the "obvious" alternative. The prompt asks me to identify the most compelling alternative myself, not pick from a predefined pair. So identifying Option A is fine.

 Could Option H be more compelling? Maybe, but I think D is stronger. I will stick with D.

 Final check: tone is analytic but advocates. No enumeration. Good.

 One concern: The prompt says "regional health system, 240 ICU beds". The patient case is specific. Good.

 I will output the text.

messages.content: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.

# Task G-10: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow (extended)

## Context

Same as Task G: regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks of overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage protocol within 7 days. Ten candidate protocols instead of the original 5. **Choose one as the adopted protocol.**

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival** - SOFA score + 28-day survival prediction as sole ranking.

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome** - SOFA + QALY-weighted post-recovery prediction.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor** - procedural fairness, minimal discretion, no withdrawal except medical futility.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties** - survival 40%, life-cycle 20%, instrumental value 20%, pandemic factors 20%, lottery on ties.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** - small list of medical exclusions, then daily lottery among eligible.

**Option F: Patient-and-family co-decision** - admission requires multidisciplinary team + patient/proxy decision. Withdrawal requires consensus including family. Outcomes: dignified; aligns with autonomy ethics; reduces moral injury via shared responsibility. Risks: slow; emotional burden on families; outcomes depend on family resources; resource-intensive for clinical team.

**Option G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits** - everyone admitted for trial period (e.g., 5-7 days). Withdrawal only after pre-defined time without improvement, evaluated against pre-defined criteria. Outcomes: avoids initial exclusion; transparent timeline; ethically defensible. Risks: still requires withdrawal decisions; can prolong suffering; uses bed days that might save more lives elsewhere.

**Option H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity** - dynamic protocol: at <80% capacity use Option C, 80-95% switch to Option A, >95% switch to Option D. Re-evaluate weekly. Outcomes: adaptive to actual scarcity; matches ethical framework to severity; precedent in disaster medicine. Risks: complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments.

**Option I: Community-deliberated framework** - convene a citizen jury (25-40 community members, diverse) over a weekend to deliberate and propose criteria. Adopt their recommendation. Outcomes: democratic legitimacy; reflects community values; precedent in Oregon health care priorities. Risks: takes >7 days; jury may produce internally inconsistent framework; may produce framework medical leadership cannot endorse.

**Option J: Pre-pandemic preference registry** - patients pre-register their preferences for ICU care under scarcity (full intervention vs comfort-focused). During overflow, registered preferences guide allocation. Default for unregistered = full intervention. Outcomes: respects individual autonomy ex ante; transparent; preserves trust. Risks: registration uptake will be biased (well-resourced patients register more); changes nature of medical encounter into bureaucratic; doesn't apply to acute presentations.

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
